front: What are the two main methods for CPU to access I/O devices?
back: Memory-Mapped I/O and Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O).

A technique where I/O devices and their registers are mapped into the same address space as the main memory. The CPU uses the same instructions to access both memory and I/O devices, treating I/O registers as memory locations.

front: What is the key characteristic of Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O)?
back: It uses a separate, dedicated address space exclusively for I/O devices. Special instructions (like IN and OUT in x86) are required to communicate with I/O ports, distinguishing them from memory accesses.

front: What is a key advantage of Programmed I/O (Polling)?
back: It is very simple to implement in both hardware and software, as it does not require complex interrupt controllers or DMA controllers.

front: What is the primary disadvantage of Programmed I/O (Polling) compared to Interrupt-Driven I/O?
back: It is inefficient because the CPU wastes time repeatedly checking (polling) the status of the I/O device, even when the device is not ready to transfer data, instead of performing other useful work.
